title: Generalized Shift Vector as the Intrinsic Dipole of Many-Body Correlated Electronic States
authors: Jiaming Hu, Sudipta Kundu, Zhichao Guo, Joshua J. P. Thompson, Wenbin Li, Hua Wang, Bartomeu Monserrat
abstract: Shift vectors play a central role in nonlinear optics and transport phenomena, where they are usually understood as charge-center shifts associated with transitions between quantum states. Here we show that the same geometric structure can be more fundamentally understood as the intrinsic dipole moment of a single correlated state. Our derivation clarifies the local and global aspects of gauge invariance, the origin of the phase-gradient term, and its connection to the internal coherence structure of many-body correlations. The single-state shift character appears both as a displacement of the real-space joint probability density and as a linear electric-field modification in energy space. Applying this framework to optically induced correlations, electron-phonon-mediated processes, and excitonic electron-hole states, we recover previously proposed shift vectors and the standard expression for the shift current as special cases. Our results establish a common physical foundation for shift vectors as intrinsic dipolar properties of correlated electronic states.
summary: This paper reinterprets the shift vector as an intrinsic dipole moment of a single many-body correlated state rather than a property of transitions between states. This unified framework encompasses various phenomena including excitonic and electron-phonon-mediated processes. It demonstrates that the shift vector captures essential phase information within many-body correlations.
main_problem: Redefining the shift vector, a key quantity in nonlinear optics and transport, from a transition-based concept between two states to a fundamental intrinsic dipole property of a single many-body correlated state.
main_result: The authors establish that the shift vector is the intrinsic dipole moment of a correlated state, providing a unified framework that recovers optical, excitonic, and electron-phonon shift vectors as special cases.
method: A theoretical derivation using many-body wavefunctions expanded in a Bloch-product basis, employing a branch-fixed approach to ensure gauge invariance and analyzing the expectation value of the electric dipole operator.
model_or_system: Many-body correlated electronic states, specifically including N-particle states such as excitons (electron-hole pairs), Cooper pairs, trions, and biexcitons in periodic systems.
key_observables: Generalized shift vector (X_S), real-space joint probability density, linear Stark effect, and DC shift current.
important_parameters: Weak-correlation limit, two-band approximation, small-patch/short-range regime, and the phase-gradient of the many-body profile function.
main_assumptions: Assumes smooth U(1) gauge transformations, neglects edge effects in extended systems, and utilizes a weak-hybridization/small-relative-momentum expansion.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows schematics of real-space probability density displacement and the linear Stark effect; Figure 2 presents first-principles results for MoS2 illustrating the internal phase structure of different exciton states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the shift vector problem, provides a many-body derivation of the generalized shift vector, proves gauge invariance, demonstrates the recovery of standard limits (optical/excitonic), and applies the framework to specific examples like MoS2 and Cooper pairs.
why_interesting: It provides a unified geometric and dipolar interpretation of many-body correlations, connecting internal phase coherence to observable transport and optical phenomena like the shift current.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematics of the physical manifestations of the exciton shift vector. (a) Real-space shift of the (coarse-grained) probability density: the maximum peak is displaced from xi −xj = 0 (solid curve) to xi −xj = −XS,Q 1 (dashed curve). (b) Linear Stark effect of the exciton under a homogeneous electrostatic field F in a finite system. The state S = 1 (S = 2) possesses a finite (vanishing) shift vec- tor, and therefore exhibits (does not exhibit) a linear field response (red line) as the leading contribution at small |F |.
